coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent N, P doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of dopamine and temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Tammina, Sai Kumar
Yang, Dezhi
Koppala, Sivasankar
Cheng, Chunsheng
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: In recent times, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an optical sensor have attained massive attention owing to their excellent optical properties. In current investigation, our group presented an easy and economical methodology to synthesize the nitrogen and phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots (N, P doped CQDs) for sensing dopamine (DA) and temperature in aqueous medium. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by using XRD, XPS, TEM, UV–Vis, FT-IR and fluorescence techniques. The N, P doped CQDs were synthesized via one-step microwave digestion method by using citric acid, ethylenediamine and urea phosphate as precursors. This method established the noble water solubility, good optical performances and fluorescence thermosensitivity of N, P doped CQDs. Also, N, P doped CQDs demonstrated a wide linear range of 10–500 μM (R2 = 0.994) and offered an electrifying detection limit of 0.021 μM for quantifying the dopamine. Moreover, this sensor possessed a good sensitivity, reversibility and linearity in the range of 10–70 °C. In addition, the CQDs sensing system repel the interference from probable foreign substances in real sample analysis, and attained good recoveries, which revealed the tremendous selectivity and adequate accuracy of the carbon quantum dots for sensing dopamine. The proposed N, P doped CQDs are simple as well as effective optical nanosensor and clasps venerable potential to widen the applications in analysis of biomolecules and other areas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, P doped CQDs
Photoluminescent
Microwave Digestion
Dopamine Detection
Temperature sensor
Real sample analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, P Doped Carbon Quantum Dots
Characterization of N, P Doped CQDs
Design Strategy for Detection of DA
Analysis of DA in Real Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of N, P doped CQDs.
XPS spectra (a) C1 s spectrum (b) N1 s spectrum (c) O1 s spectrum (d) P1 s spectrum.
FT-IR spectrum of N, P doped CQDs.
TEM images of N, P doped CQDs and particle size distribution (inset figure).
EDX micrograph of N, P doped CQDs.
Elemental mapping along with EDX micrograph of N, P doped CQDs (a) Nitrogen (b) carbon (c) Oxygen (d) Phosphorous (f) EDX micrograph.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of N, P doped CQD's (a) UV–Vis spectrum (1) under UV radiation (2) under visible radiations (b) emission intensities of CQDs at different excitations (c) excitation and emission spectra of CQDs.
UV–Vis (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQDs, CQDs-DA, CQDs-Ag+, CQDs-DA-Ag+.
(a) Plot of F/F0 Vs. Concentration of DA. (b) Fluorescence quenching CQDs-Ag+ probe with various concentrations of DA.

Thermal sensitivity of CQDs in the presence of DA (a) Temperature effect on fluorescence emission of CQDs from 10 to 70 °C with an excitation of 350 nm. CQDs fluorescence intensities variation while heating (b) and (c) cooling (d) reversible temperature-based fluorescence of the N, P doped CQDs.
(a) Effect of pH on fluorescence quenching of (a) CQDs and (b) DA (500 μM) to the CQDs.
Fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the presence of DA (500 μM) at various time intervals.
The linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching and DA concentration (10–500 μM), Inset: Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs in the presence of DA (10–500 μM).
Selectivity of CQDs to the various interfering molecules with concentration of 200 μM.
Comparison of N, P doped CQDs QY with the reported literature.
Comparison of methods for DA determination.
Determination of DA in serum and urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent N, P doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of dopamine and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Metallurgical and Energy Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Province Food Safety Research Institute, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Province Food Safety Research Institute
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent times, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an optical sensor have attained massive attention owing to their excellent optical properties. In current investigation, our group presented an easy and economical methodology to synthesize the nitrogen and phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots (N, P doped CQDs) for sensing dopamine (DA) and temperature in aqueous medium. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by using XRD, XPS, TEM, UV–Vis, FT-IR and fluorescence techniques. The N, P doped CQDs were synthesized via one-step microwave digestion method by using citric acid, ethylenediamine and urea phosphate as precursors. This method established the noble water solubility, good optical performances and fluorescence thermosensitivity of N, P doped CQDs. Also, N, P doped CQDs demonstrated a wide linear range of 10–500 μM (R2 = 0.994) and offered an electrifying detection limit of 0.021 μM for quantifying the dopamine. Moreover, this sensor possessed a good sensitivity, reversibility and linearity in the range of 10–70 °C. In addition, the CQDs sensing system repel the interference from probable foreign substances in real sample analysis, and attained good recoveries, which revealed the tremendous selectivity and adequate accuracy of the carbon quantum dots for sensing dopamine. The proposed N, P doped CQDs are simple as well as effective optical nanosensor and clasps venerable potential to widen the applications in analysis of biomolecules and other areas.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), is one of the vital neurotransmitters in the humanoid central nervous system, has stimulated the substantial curiosity because of its latent therapeutics for psychiatric and neurological disorders [1,2]. Usually DA (Fig. 1 ) accumulates in brain before it releases [3] and normal levels of DA helps in maintaining the proper blood pressure, motivation, learning, physical activity and awareness [4,5]. Also, Lewis et al. attested the role of DA as a neuroprotective agent against Parkinson's disease [6]. Furthermore, few other movement related disorders such as Ekbom's restless syndrome are also correlated to the irregular concentration of DA [7,8]. Till today, there is no easily operated DA sensor for clinical use [9]. Thus, an accurate and sensitive way for the detection of DA in the diagnostics of pertinent mental diseases is enormously needed. Various methods have been reported to identify the concentration of DA in human bio fluids such as colorimetric methods [10], electrochemical methods [11], flow injection analysis [12], HPLC [13] and chemiluminiscence [14]. Even though these methods are advantageous but also few precincts are there, which includes difficult methods, pre-treatment with costly reagents and extensive time is necessary to complete the analysis. The DA sensing especially with electrochemical technique is a huge problem because the electrochemical potential of DA coincides with the electrochemical potential of co-existing species such as uric acid and ascorbic acid [14–16]. But fluorescence spectrophotometer has its own advantages for sensing or detection applications, because this technique is not demanding the costly reagents, difficult methods, sample pre-treatment. However, this technique is cost-effective but the generated results are precise and accurate.

The topical advances in analytical chemistry has demonstrated that nanomaterials could be an ultimate source for constructing chemical sensors, nanomaterials include silver or gold nanoparticles, quantum dots etc., showed a significant optical responses which are novel with reference to their bulk counterparts. Besides, one can regulate the electromagnetic, optical and remaining properties by tuning size through quantum confinement effect [17]. Further modifications can bring out by making core-shell moieties or by capping the surface-bound molecules [18–20]. In recent times, the development of carbon dots (CDs) has created huge excitement because of their pronounced potential in optoelectronic devices [21], photocatalysis [22], bio imaging [23–24] biosensors [25–27] and analytical applications [28–30]. Usually, CDs are <10 nm in size and possess few worthwhile properties such as fluorescence, biocompatibility, tremendous up conversion, extraordinary photostability and less toxicity, which makes them auspicious alternative for semiconductor quantum dots in the biological and chemical analysis. However, the particular reason for this luminescence is still in debate, it is guessed that emissive traps [31], quantum confinement effect [32], free zig-zag sites [33], and aromatic structures [34] contribute to their fluorescence emission. So far, the synthetic chemistry of CDs have been investigated through various approaches such as laser ablation [31], hydrothermal treatment [35,36], strong acid oxidation [37], electrochemical method [38], pyrolysis of carbon precursors [39], discharge [40] and microwave methods [41,42]. Related to the other synthetic methods, microwave assisted method is simple, cost-effective, short reaction time and the aptitude to produce a variety of CDs in a solitary step [43].

Temperature is a one of the physical parameters, which influences a lot on the biological and chemical systems. The pervasive applications demand the accurate determination of temperature which has great importance [44]. As yet, numerous materials have been developed for the temperature detection such as raman spectroscopy, scanning probe microscopy and fluorescence based measurements [45–47]. In fact, various nanomaterials with fluorescence properties also have been reported for temperature determination including organic dyes, fluorescent polymers and semiconductor quantum dots [48–50].

Herein, we developed a facile microwave assisted technique to fabricate the nitrogen (N) and phosphorous (P) doped carbon quantum dots (N, P doped CQDs) by using citric acid, ethylenediamine and urea phosphate as precursors short of further surface modification. The N, P doped CQDs demonstrated the narrow size distribution, tremendous water-solubility, significant temperature-dependent fluorescence and high fluorescence quantum yield (44.2%). Also, it showed the remarkable potential in dopamine (DA) as well as temperature detection. As a highly luminescent fluorescent probe for detection of DA, the N, P doped CQDs shown the exceptional selectivity and sensitivity with the linear range of 0.1-500 μM (R2 = 0.994) and with detection limit of 0.021 μM. As a temperature sensor, the fluorescence intensity linearly declined within the range from 10 to 70 °C with excellent sensitivity and substantial reversibility.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). citric acid, Na3PO4 and Na2HPO4 were bought from the Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China. All the biomolecules used for selectivity were provided by Xinxing Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. China. Every chemicals was used without further purification.1.0 mM DA stock solution was prepared by dissolving it in water and diluted to the scale line and stored for further use at 4 °C. Human serum sample was collected from Yunnan Hospital located in Kunming. Whole experiments were carried out in ultra-pure water. CQDs synthesis was performed in microwave digestion system (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses was performed in X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK).The emission of CQDs were recorded using Agilent G9800A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (USA). Absorption changes were measured in Shimadzu, UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Japan) and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was recorded on a TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) and.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, P Doped Carbon Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: N, P doped CQDs were synthesized via a one-step microwave digestion system (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German). Briefly, 0.2 g urea phosphate, 0.3 g citric acid and 875 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water to form a clear solution under ultrasound. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a 60 mL Teflon microwave vessel, vessel was tightly sealed and placed in microwave oven. The temperature of the reaction was risen to 150 °C in 1 min using 850 W microwave irradiations. Reaction was proceeded at this temperature for 30 min through controlling the duration of microwave irradiation (700 W). After cooling down to the room temperature, the obtained transparent solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min followed by filtration through a 0.22 m membrane and the obtained CQDs solution was keptin refrigerator at 4 °C for further applications. A control sample was also synthesized without adding the ethylenediamine and urea phosphate under the same experimental conditions.

CQDs quantum yield was measured using previously reported method [51]. For calculation of QY of CQDs quinine sulfate taken as reference compound, which is having QY of 0.53 as per the reports and optical density of the CQDs taken as a sample and fixed to <0.1 in water at the 360 nm wavelength. The reference compound was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (g = 1.33). Emission spectra were recorded for both reference and CQDs at 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs calculated using the following equation
(1)
Q =
Q R
I S
I R
A R
A S
η S 2
η R 2
where subscript ‘S’ and ‘R’ refers to the sample and reference. The symbol η refers to the solvent refraction index and A indicates the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and I refers the integrated emission intensity.

CQDs solution (36 μL) was diluted with 2 mL of distilled water and to this 2 mL of various concentrations of DA solution were added. This mixture was vertexed for few mins for thorough mixing of the solution. After 5 min incubation at room temperature, fluorescence intensity was recorded at 350 nm excitation and emission was observed at 441 nm.

Human blood sample (provided by Yunnan Hospital of Kunming) was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 mins to separate the serum. Then the supernatant layer was collected, labelled as serum and stored at 4 °C for further use. Urine sample (from laboratory members) was decoloured by adding little amount of activated charcoal, followed by centrifugation to eradicate maximum amount of unwanted components and diluted it 10-fold before experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, P Doped CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 represents the diffraction pattern of synthesized N, P doped CQDs from 0 to 80°. The XRD patterns of CQDs shows a resolvable broad peak centred at 25. 8°, which is close to the diffraction peak of the graphite (002) crystal plane at 26.1° [51]. Therefore, all of the above results demonstrate the existence of CQDs.

The functional groups of CQDs were investigated using XPS and FT-IR spectrophotometer. As displayed in Fig. 3(a) C1 s spectrum could be divided into five major peaks which are corresponds to CC (283.5 eV), CC (284 eV), CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-O (285.6 eV) and C=O/C=N (287.42 eV) functional groups respectively [52]. Fig. 3(b) represents the deconvoluted N1 s spectra, which shown the three relative nitrogen species of N-C-N (398.86 eV) and NH (399.93 eV) [53]. Fig. 3(c) corresponds to the deconvoluted spectra of O1s, the peaks observed at 530.66 eV and 531.6385 represents the C-OH and CO respectively [53]. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the peaks at132.25 eV and 133.18 eV corresponds to PO and PO functional groups respectively [54,55]. This observations were further confirmed with the FT-IR report (Fig. 4 ). The peaks at 3470 cm−1and 3200 cm−1due to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH functional groups indicating their presence on the surface of CQDs and its presence could promote the hydrophilic nature of CQDs. The CH stretching vibrations are observed at 2940 and 2760 cm−1 [56,57]. Also, stretching vibrations of CO, CC and CN are observed at 1870, 1570 and 1390 cm−1 respectively. The PO, P-OH, and PC stretching vibrations are found at 1080, 828 and 697 cm−1 respectively [58]. These observations are in well agreement with the XPS results and concluding that the synthesized CQDs are functionalized with nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous groups.

Fig. 5 displayed the TEM image of the CQDs. As shown in figure majority of the CQDs are in spherical shape and most of them are in between 2 and 4 nm size (inset figure). The average diameter of the CQDs are 2.7 ± 0.6 nm. This observation confirms the narrow size distribution and near uniform morphology of the CQDs.

Figs. 6 and 7 shows the EDX and elemental mapping of the CQDs. These two characterizations were performed to investigate the elemental composition and spatial distribution of the synthesized CQDs. The micrograph is displaying the Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), Oxygen (O) and Phosphorous (P) elements, the atomic and weight percentage of N, C, O and P are 37.78, 36.62, 25.02, 0.58 and 38.15, 31.71, 28.85, 1.29 respectively. Fig. 7 (a–f) shows the spatial distribution of Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen and Phosphorous elements. The images are revealing that the nitrogen is distributed in higher concentration than carbon and oxygen in a predefined area. In case of phosphorous the spatial distribution is lesser compared to remaining elements. The spatial distribution of each element can be expressed quantitatively, thus the atomic and weight percentage of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous are 33.16, 35.42, 30.44, 0.98 and 28.21, 35.14, 34.50, 2.16 respectively.

Few authors reported that the electron transport occurs between the dopamine and CQDs and this phenomenon ensures either in the presence of oxygen or in alkaline environment [68]. Under these experimental conditions DA oxidizes to dopamine-quinone, further the formed dopamine-quinone can quench the fluorescence of the CQDs. The reason behind the quenching is that the electron acceptor alters the recombination kinetics of the accepted electron and hole of photoexcited CQDs. This phenomenon is well matched with the reported literature [69–71]. In addition, CQDs functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups, these groups interact with the DA through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. Fig. 9 demonstrate the plausible quenching mechanism of DA, DA oxidizes to dopamine-quinone, followed by acceptance of electron from the CQDs results in quenching of CQDs fluorescence.

The spectral characteristic of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) gave lead to establish a fluorescent probe as CQDs-Ag+ for the sensitive and selective detection of dopamine. Fig. 10 (a) & (b) shows the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of CQDs combination with DA and Ag+. One can observe that Ag+ alone could not quench the fluorescence intensity but after addition of DA, the apparent quenching of fluorescence happened (Fig. 10(b)). The actual reason behind this phenomenon is that DA owns strong reducing property and it can reduce the AgNO3 solution to AgNPs under weakly alkaline conditions [72]. Thus, if CQDs-Ag+ complex is treated as a fluorescent probe, addition of DA leads to formation of AgNPs and it further quenches the fluorescence intensity of CQDs (Fig. 11a). As shown in Fig. 10a there is no indication of absorbance for CQDs-Ag+ at 467 nm but after addition of DA to the CQDs + Ag+, leads to the strong absorbance at 467 nm, indicating the formation of AgNPs. Thus in-situ development of AgNPs are crucial for CQDs-Ag+ based determination of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design Strategy for Detection of DA

ce:section.ce:para: This investigation was performed to explore the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. From 10 °C to 70 °C the fluorescence changes were recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer. The CQDs solution was exposed for 5 mins at each temperature to observe the changes in fluorescence intensity and the observed reports explored the potential of CQDs as a temperature sensor, because the raise in temperature from low to high results in continuous quenching of fluorescence intensity. Fig. 12a–c display the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of N, P doped CQDs. The (F/F0) value declined with increasing temperature (R2 = 0.9928) and increased with decreasing temperature (R2 = 0.9979).This decreasing phenomenon might be attributed to the aggregation of CQDs, aggregation followed by quenching of CQDs fluorescence [73]. Fig. 12(a) shows the quenching pattern of fluorescence and inset figure illustrate the good relationship amid the declining fluorescence intensity Vs rising temperature, Fig. 12(b) and (c) shows the linear relationship between quenching efficiency (F/F0) and temperature, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N, P doped CQDs at 10 °C and elevated temperatures respectively. This result illustrate that N, P doped CQDs inherent the reversible and restorable fluorescence property with heating and cooling temperatures. As per the previous reports [74,75] the reason behind this phenomenon due to the relaxation of photoexcited electron such as radiative relaxation, non-radiative relaxation and thermally activated trap on surface and impurity states during the heating process, while re-cooling the CQDs exhibits the opposite trend. This reversible eccentric recommend that N, P doped CQDs can implement self-reference nanothermometry and acquired the required potential to utilize this CQDs as a temperature sensor.

The fluorescence intensity of CQDs was studied in the presence of DA at different time intervals. Fig. 14 displays the fluorescence quenching of CQDs after the addition of DA and after 2 min of reaction stable fluorescence was observed. Even though the stable fluorescence was observed, further the reaction time was extended for >30 min. The fluorescence stability of CQDs are further checked after 30 days, there was no change in fluorescence intensity of CQDs (data not shown). This observation reveals that, synthesis method is rapid and stable for detection of DA.

Fig. 16 exhibits the changes of fluorescence intensity of N, P doped CQDs against various biomolecules. Selectivity was performed to evaluate the fluorescence interference of CQDs in the presence of various biomolecules which includes L-tyr, SA, L-gly, Suc, Lac, L-gly, L-cys, L-asp, L-glutamic, L-tyr, UA, AA and DA. Among all these biomolecules only DA could capably quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs, whereas the other interfering substances could not quench the fluorescence effectively and also change in fluorescence intensity was insignificant. Thus the observed selectivity results confirms that the CQDs can be utilized for the detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DA in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para:
DA in serum and urine samples were detected to gauze the practicality of the method. After assessment of sensitivity and selectivity of CQDs against DA, the detection studies were further extended for real samples analysis in human serum and urine samples. For this investigation different concentrations of DA were spiked in three plasma and urine samples. Then the recovery percentage was calculated using the below mentioned formulae. As displayed in Table 3 the precision of their analytical strategy is acceptable.
(2)
% Recovery =
C 1
/
C 0
× 100
here C1 is takes the sample value and C0 takes the blank value. The observed result illustrate that the N, P doped CQDs can be utilized for detection of DA in real samples.

In summary, we synthesized a highly fluorescent N, P doped CQDs with a good quantum yield of 44% in a simple as well as greener way through one step microwave treatment and explored it as a fluorescent probe for the detection of temperature and DA. CQDs exhibited tremendous optical performance, notable fluorescence, excellent water solubility and thermosensitivity. The projected nanosensor evident the reversible and restorable fluorescence properties towards temperature. The method is magnificently imposed in the detection of DA in serum and urine samples with good limit of detection values, it is indicating that our method is appropriate for the detection of DA in real samples. This work could be a significant step for carbon based materials to extend the investigation and application for the temperature and dopamine sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology Sai Kumar Tammina greatly acknowledges the Kunming University of Science and Technology Post-Doctoral fund (No. 10988869). Sivasankar Koppala acknowledges the Kunming University of Science and Technology Post-Doctoral fund (No. 10988804) and Yunnan province Post-Doctoral Training Fund (No. 10988833).
